section: Chapter 8: Force and Laws of Motion

concepts.lesson: Force is a push or pull that can change the state of motion of an object. It can start motion, stop motion, change speed, change direction, or change the shape of an object.
concepts.real_example: Pushing a trolley in a supermarket makes it move.
key_points: Force is a push or pull
Changes motion or shape of objects

concepts.lesson: Balanced forces occur when two equal forces act in opposite directions on an object. In this situation, the object does not change its state of rest or motion.
concepts.real_example: Two people pushing a box equally from opposite sides keeps the box stationary.
key_points: Object remains at rest or continues uniform motion

concepts.lesson: Unbalanced forces occur when the forces acting on an object are not equal. These forces cause a change in the state of motion of the object.
concepts.real_example: A football moves when a player kicks it.

concepts.lesson: Inertia is the natural tendency of an object to resist a change in its state of rest or motion. Heavier objects usually have greater inertia.
concepts.real_example: Passengers move forward when a moving bus stops suddenly.
key_points: Types: inertia of rest, motion, direction

concepts.lesson: Newton's First Law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force. This law is also called the Law of Inertia.
concepts.real_example: A book placed on a table remains at rest until someone pushes it.
key_points: Object stays at rest or uniform motion
Requires unbalanced force to change motion

concepts.lesson: Momentum is the quantity of motion possessed by an object. It is defined as the product of mass and velocity.
concepts.real_example: A moving truck has more momentum than a bicycle because it has greater mass.

concepts.lesson: Newton's Second Law states that the rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied force and occurs in the direction of the force. This law gives the relation F = ma.
concepts.real_example: A football accelerates more when kicked harder.

concepts.lesson: The SI unit of force is Newton (N). One Newton is defined as the force required to produce an acceleration of 1 m/s² in an object of mass 1 kg.
concepts.real_example: Applying a small force to move a 1 kg object slightly represents about one Newton.

concepts.lesson: Newton's Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. These forces act on different objects simultaneously.
concepts.real_example: When a swimmer pushes water backward, the swimmer moves forward.

concepts.lesson: Action and reaction forces are pairs of forces that occur simultaneously when two objects interact. They are always equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
concepts.real_example: A gun recoils backward when a bullet is fired forward.
